text: The surgeon performs a peritumoral injection of indocyanine green (ICG) into the subsorosal layer, which is visualized passing to the drain and leaf nodes within fifteen minutes. Under near-infrared light, the fluorescence emitted by ICG reveals the lymphatic drainage patterns of the tumor, accurately showing left colic drainage. The surgical field is prepared for a left hemicolectomy, with ligation of both the left colic and the left branch of the middle colic arteries.

The surgeon employs an infracolic three-step approach to mobilize the splenic flexure using a single jockeying technique. Special camera filters are utilized to visualize ICG fluorescence, allowing the surgeon to switch between normal and fluorescence viewing modes. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is ligated using robotic hemologs, and the tail of the pancreas is identified and separated from the splenic flexure of the colon.

The surgeon divides the mesentery up to the level of the small bowel and colonic transection. Indocyanine green (ICG) is injected to assess the blood supply at both ends of the bowel near the transection site. Using robotic assistance, an intracorporeal anastomosis is performed, followed by continuous suturing to close the enterotomy. A second layer of continuous running sutures is applied to reinforce the anastomosis.
